ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

Downstream writers will use the query_postgres tool to inspect source data while implementing your plan.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 23, 15, 440145, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_src"."Account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_src.Account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text NULL
- `Customer_Tier__c` text NULL
- `Region__c` text NULL
- `Industry` text NULL
- `Website` text NULL
- `BillingCity` text NULL
- `BillingCountry` text NULL
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_src.Contact  (315 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text NULL
- `LastName` text NULL
- `Email` text NULL
- `Phone` text NULL
- `Title` text NULL
- `Role__c` text NULL
- `Preferred_Language__c` text NULL
- `AccountId` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_src.Installed_Asset__c  (600 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text NULL
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text NULL
- `Account__c` text NULL
- `Project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_src.Opportunity  (364 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NULL
- `StageName` text NULL
- `CloseDate` text NULL
- `Amount` text NULL
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text NULL
- `AccountId` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_src.Project__c  (196 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text NULL
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text NULL
- `Account__c` text NULL
- `Opportunity__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 23, 15, 440150, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 23, 15, 440277, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f0f41-c16f-710e-b17d-612cd4a8153d', conversation_id='019f0f41-c16f-710e-b17d-612ba47c4d5e')

data.overview: A global plan for mapping source data from `fixture_messy_data_src` to the target schema. All IDs are assumed to be direct mappings. Foreign key relationships are maintained directly. Missing target columns like CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, and IsDeleted are handled with NULLs or sensible defaults. Enum values are normalized to match target domain with NULL fallback. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 23, 50, 665271, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_src"."Account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_src.Account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text NULL
- `Customer_Tier__c` text NULL
- `Region__c` text NULL
- `Industry` text NULL
- `Website` text NULL
- `BillingCity` text NULL
- `BillingCountry` text NULL
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_src.Contact  (315 rows)

Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text NULL
- `LastName` text NULL
- `Email` text NULL
- `Phone` text NULL
- `Title` text NULL

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

    Opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    NULL AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.StageName)) = 'CLOSED LOST' THEN 'Closed Lost'

            WHEN TRIM(source."CloseDate") ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(source."CloseDate"), 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(source."CloseDate") ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(source."CloseDate"), 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        REPLACE(
            REPLACE(
                REPLACE(
                    REPLACE(
                        REPLACE(
                            TRIM(source.Amount),
                            '€', ''
                        ),
                        '$', ''
                    ),
                    'EUR ', ''
                ),
                '.', ''
            ),
            ',', '.'
        ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    TRIM(source.CurrencyIsoCode) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    source.AccountId AS "AccountId",
    NULL AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

```sql
    TO_CHAR(
        CASE
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'YYYYMMDD')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        'YYYY-MM-DD'
    ) AS "Warranty_End_Date__c",
    TRIM(asset.Account__c) AS "Account__c",
    TRIM(asset.Project__c) AS "Project__c",
    NULL::text AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
```

```sql
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",

FROM
```

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.

- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args={'reasoning': 'The primary fix involves correctly quoting all mixed-case source column names like "Id", "Name", and "Industry" to resolve PostgreSQL's case-sensitivity for unquoted identifiers. The transformations for `Name` (COALESCE with `Id`), `Customer_Tier__c` (CASE statement for enum mapping), and default values for `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, and `IsDeleted` remain consistent with the target schema and transformation guidelines.', 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    "Id" AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM("Name"), "Id") AS "Name",
    TRIM("ERP_Number__c") AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE UPPER(TRIM("Customer_Tier__c"))
        WHEN 'GOLD' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN 'SILVER' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN 'BRONZE' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN 'PLATINUM' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM("Region__c") AS "Region__c",
    TRIM("Industry") AS "Industry",
    TRIM("Website") AS "Website",
    TRIM("BillingCity") AS "BillingCity",
    TRIM("BillingCountry") AS "BillingCountry",
    TRIM("Legacy_Customer_ID__c") AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 25, 14, 325874, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    source.id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(source.name), source.id) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'PROSPECTING' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'QUALIFICATION' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'NEEDS ANALYSIS' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'VALUE PROPOSITION' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'ID. DECISION MAKERS' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'PERCEPTION ANALYSIS' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'PROPOSAL/PRICE QUOTE' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'NEGOTIATION/REVIEW' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'CLOSED WON' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(source.stagename)) = 'CLOSED LOST' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(source.closedate) IS NULL OR TRIM(source.closedate) IN ('0000-00-00', 'N/A', '') THEN NULL
            WHEN TRIM(source.closedate) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TRIM(source.closedate)::date, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(source.closedate) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(source.closedate), 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(source.closedate) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(source.closedate), 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(source.closedate) ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(TRIM(source.closedate), 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        '1900-01-01'
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        REPLACE(
            REPLACE(
                REPLACE(
                    REPLACE(
                        REPLACE(
                            TRIM(source.amount),
                            '€', ''
                        ),
                        '$', ''
                    ),
                    'EUR ', ''
                ),
                '.', ''
            ),
            ',', '.'
        ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    TRIM(source.currencyisocode) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    source.accountid AS "AccountId",
    NULL AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_messy_data_src', 'Opportunity') }} AS source

```

- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 25, 14, 325936, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Installed_Asset__c` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    TRIM(asset.Id) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(asset.Name), TRIM(asset.Id)) AS "Name",
    TRIM(asset.Serial_Number__c) AS "Serial_Number__c",
    TO_CHAR(
        CASE
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'YYYYMMDD')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A') ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(NULLIF(TRIM(asset."Warranty_End_Date__c"), 'N/A'), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        'YYYY-MM-DD'
    ) AS "Warranty_End_Date__c",
    TRIM(asset.Account__c) AS "Account__c",
    TRIM(asset.Project__c) AS "Project__c",
    NULL::text AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_messy_data_src', 'Installed_Asset__c') }} AS asset

```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 25, 44, 640389, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Installed_Asset__c` failed.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 26, 12, 499071, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Project__c` failed.

```sql
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'COMPLETED' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'IN PLANNING' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'ON HOLD' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'CANCELLED' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
```
    src."Account__c" AS "Account__c",
    src."Opportunity__c" AS "Opportunity__c",
    NULL AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_messy_data_src', 'Project__c') }} AS src


- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    src.Id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(src.Name), src.Id) AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'COMPLETED' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) IN ('IN PLANNING', 'IN BEARBEITUNG', 'PENDING') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'ON HOLD' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src."Project_Status__c")) = 'CANCELLED' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" = '0000-00-00' THEN NULL -- Handle invalid zero date
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') -- New format for DD.MM.YYYY
        WHEN src."Go_Live_Date__c" ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src."Go_Live_Date__c", 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    src."Account__c" AS "Account__c",
    src."Opportunity__c" AS "Opportunity__c",
    NULL AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_messy_data_src', 'Project__c') }} AS src

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

Output is ISO YYYY-MM-DD text.", 'source_columns': ['CloseDate'], 'target_column': 'CloseDate'}, {'explanation': "Mapped from source 'Amount'. Removes 'EUR ' prefix and spaces. Detects European (comma as decimal) vs. standard (dot as decimal) format and converts to DOUBLE PRECISION.", 'target_column': 'Amount', 'source_columns': ['Amount']}, {'explanation': "Direct mapping from the source 'CurrencyIsoCode' column.", 'source_columns': ['CurrencyIsoCode'], 'target_column': 'CurrencyIsoCode'}, {'explanation': "Direct mapping from the source 'AccountId' column.", 'target_column': 'AccountId', 'source_columns': ['AccountId']}, {'source_columns': [], 'target_column': 'Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c', 'explanation': 'Populated with NULL as there is no corresponding source column.'}, {'explanation': 'Populated with NULL as there is no corresponding source column.', 'source_columns': [], 'target_column': 'CreatedDate'}, {'explanation': 'Populated with NULL as there is no corresponding source column.', 'target_column': 'LastModifiedDate', 'source_columns': []}, {'source_columns': [], 'target_column': 'IsDeleted', 'explanation': 'Hardcoded to 0 as there is no corresponding source column and the target type is integer.'}], 'reasoning': 'This model transforms the raw Opportunity data by mapping columns, handling NULL constraints for Id, Name, StageName, and CloseDate, and normalizing data types for Amount and StageName. Default values are provided for missing required fields or unparseable data, ensuring compliance with the target schema and data quality rules.', 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    op."Id" AS "Id",
    COALESCE(op."Name", 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    CASE LOWER(TRIM(op."StageName"))
        WHEN 'prospecting' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN 'prospect' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN 'in kontakt' THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN 'qualification' THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN 'quali' THEN 'Qualification гибкий план' THEN 'Qualification' -- Assuming this is a typo and should be Qualification
        WHEN 'needs analysis' THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN 'value proposition' THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN 'id. decision makers' THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN 'perception analysis' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN 'in prüfung' THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN 'proposal/price quote' THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN 'negotiation/review' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN 'closed won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'won' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'gewonnen' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)' THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN 'closed lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN 'lost' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN 'verloren' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN 'abgeschlossen (verloren)' THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN op."CloseDate" ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN CAST(op."CloseDate" AS DATE)
            WHEN op."CloseDate" ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(op."CloseDate", 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN op."CloseDate" ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(op."CloseDate", 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN op."CloseDate" ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(op."CloseDate", 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        '1900-01-01'::DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        CASE
            WHEN op."Amount" IS NULL THEN NULL
            ELSE
                CASE
                    WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM REGEXP_REPLACE(op."Amount", ' ', '', 'g')), '[0-9]', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN
                        -- Contains a comma, assume European format: remove dots, then replace comma with dot
                        REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM REGEXP_REPLACE(op."Amount", ' ', '', 'g')), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                    ELSE
                        -- No comma, assume standard format
                        TRIM(LEADING 'EUR ' FROM REGEXP_REPLACE(op."Amount", ' ', '', 'g'))
                END
        END AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    op."CurrencyIsoCode" AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    op."AccountId" AS "AccountId",
    NULL::TEXT AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_messy_data_src', 'Opportunity') }} AS op
', 'target_table': 'Opportunity'}, tool_call_id='pyd_ai_7764e54b60294040b7f077a09331ce0f', provider_name='google-cloud', provider_details={'thought_signature': ''})], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=4975, cache_read_tokens=4182, output_tokens=6059, details={'cached_content_tokens': 4182, 'thoughts_tokens': 4784, 'text_prompt_tokens': 4975, 'text_cache_tokens': 4182, 'text_candidates_tokens': 1275}), model_name='gemini-2.5-flash', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 26, 52, 866873, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='google-cloud', provider_url='https://aiplatform.googleapis.com/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'STOP', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 28, 17, 26, 24, 185472, tzinfo=TzInfo(0)), 'traffic_type': 'ON_DEMAND'}, provider_response_id='QFlBaoCpC8uDxs0PrZTS8Qs', finish_reason='stop')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

```sql
    TRIM("Legacy_Customer_ID__c") AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",

    0 AS "IsDeleted"
```

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable.
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`).
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.
